speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: I told you I didn't remember the star. I remember the well in the middle of the house. The old palms. Yeah, we had the old there. Everybody used this. Now this Kate Waldron that we were speaking of, she lived up at Slat Town with us too. And she had two children.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: And then there was the crystals that are cranes in Bovil now, Helen Crange. She lived up there with us too. And this mug for real, her family lived up there at Slat Town. Another one I was thinking that she might get information off or raised in Harvard. I can't remember Ruby's name. Ruby Canfield, her name used to be. Wheeler. No. Yeah, it was Queener. Yeah, oh yeah, it says Queener.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Have you gotten to tell you how to turn?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Well this crystal is an aunt of theirs. This crystal that you slip up in Slat Town. Ruby's their aunt. Ruby's their aunt. Yeah, so she was a real shark. Well that was good that you got to touch her. She could remember a lot. We used to go down there when we were kids. Yeah, we used to go down there and stay at the farm. I thought that was a great deal, boy. Yeah, it was.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: So your father happened to be there when that fire started?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: They were on the railroad working someplace because he brought the train that took us there on the flat cars.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: And wasn't there eleven? They said there were eleven flat cars?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: I don't know. I don't know. There was a lot of flat cars and everybody was... Well they thought Bobo was going to burn too, see. And then it bypassed it. See they got under control. But they thought it was going to burn too.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How come you had no warning?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: How come it... Well it was warned. I mean we knew the fire was burning from our place. And my dad had this whole wood shed split with all... All his winter woods split. He said he felt worth about that, but anyway, you know, it was all that wood that was already split. But they could see it good. But as I say, the reason they thought it was going the other way and it was all under control.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: You know where Beals Butte, is, don't you? Can't you see?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Well that's that. And see, when that was finished burning, that was completely black. There wasn't a tree or anything on that for years and years. And it just amazes me. And I go up there and I don't know how green it's getting again and everything. Because there was nothing there after that fire. And there wasn't any homes. They were all burnt. Even that log cabin across the track that Nanny and Dave Gentry lived in was burnt.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: But the shops didn't burn at all. The shops didn't burn. Didn't we just kind of burnt... Just jumped right over them. Just kind of burnt in that one area. And didn't burn the shops. And it didn't burn the school halls. That was over kind of right straight across the shops.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What did you kids grab when you left? Did you grab anything?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Well, we waunted to... Grace and I, we never were much to go barefooted anyway. And we came and Mother thought we should go barefooted in the summertime and save our shoes. And so we came in and waunted to put our shoes on. And then we had our shoes on and that's the only thing. And the clothes we had almost, that's all we had. And our brothers didn't even have shoes on. They didn't even have shoes. They didn't even have shoes. They didn't even have shoes.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: And we went to... Some people got off all along the way. We had friends. Some had friends in Derry and Avon and all of that. And we went through the potlatch because we had friends down there. And those lovely people that put up with us. Mr. and Mrs. George Fulton. She just died a few months ago, I see on the paper.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Where'd you move back after that? Did they build something?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Oh, we had a home. Our father owned a home at Bogle, Idaho. At Bogle, see. And he was renting it.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Well, then when our place up at Slaptown burned, why then, when we came back from Potlatch, we stayed there seven days, I think, in Potlatch, wasn't it?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: And then we came back, and we didn't have any furniture, but you know how people are in a small town. They all help. And then our grandmother stayed with us. And our parents came up here to the Northwestern Trading Company. It's Bergen's now. And bought our furniture and brought it back to...

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What was the law?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: He said, everything in this house is just my age. And she was right. Because she was born in September after this fire in August. So we had that house, and that's where we lived. And we just moved into the house. So we were lucky. Some people didn't have any... Oh, most of them pitched tents, you know. Pitched, you know, tents. Things to build a little shaunty or something.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Because actually all the homes were really kind of shaunties, weren't they?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Yeah, we held basements. We had one big room, and then our dad had built a little lean -to on for our bedroom. You know. Up at Slaptown. Yeah. How did that name...

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: do you know? What? Do you know how it got that name? What, Slaptown?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Oh, I suppose. Because everybody just put up a bunch of slabs, you know. I suppose. You know.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Who gave it to me?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: And just up a little bit farther was Collins, you know. They could just well call it Collins. But just this one little area there, they call Slaptown.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: I don't know why.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Why did they do that?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: I wish I could remember a lot of other things, but when I... you know. I can't think of a part of it. When your kids, you know, the things you do remember, most of them are trivial. You know, I mean, you remember something. Except, of course, something like this impressed us. But most of the things you remember when you're small, they really aren't importaunt things. I remember that Mrs. Driscoll and Troy wrote to me one time. And waunted me to give a picture, a mental picture, because she was doing this painting on plates of all the towns that had disappeared in the area. But it wouldn't have been off that ache. I couldn't remember that much.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: On Slaptown?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Mm -hmm. Yeah.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Because she's painting all the... you probably know. Well, the track went on through. You probably read her book, too. There was one, Crystal's House and our house was right down that way. And then there was the road, and up above that was Waldron's and Hughes's. And then there was that great big barn back there.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: I remember that.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: I remember that. They'd log a barn. And then there was... But let's say I couldn't... it's not that clear in my mind. You know. To paint it. To paint it or anything. So I suggested to her that she contact Maud Freel. Because she and I... Yeah. She lived up there. Because she lived there. And because when you're six years old and seven years old...

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Do they have a little school there?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Yes. A one -room schoolhouse. They had them in the first of eighth grade. That was my first... our first year of school was there. Okay. We went two years there.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Huh?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: We went two years there.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: No, we went one year there, don't you remember?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: And then we went back to... I guess we did go two years there. And we were supposed to go in the third grade when we went to Bovill. And they told us how mean the teacher was. So Grace and I put ourselves back in the second grade. Mother didn't know anything about it. No, we went two years there. Because I can tell by my age. Because I started to... I started... I was alone. So when she went to school, Mother let me go too. But I was only five. And the fire was... I was just barely seven. I must have gone to school. I was trying to think. Miss Crawford is our teacher. Miss Crawford that used to own the Bovill drugstore was our teacher. Our first teacher. Our first teacher.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: And every year when you start to school, she said, Do you like this teacher as well as you did me?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: She died a few years ago. Yes.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: She was what, 91 or something?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: 91, something.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was she a pretty good teacher?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: She was a nice teacher. She had terrible... She had so much hair and such long heavy hair. She had such terrific headaches. So she had this... This Hughes girl.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What was her name? Was it Crystal Hughes?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: It was Ma Phil's cousin. That's Butler. You're assuming it was Butler. I think it was Crystal Hughes. Ma Hughes is... Anyway, she had her teaching us all the time.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: She was an eighth grader, see?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: And she had these terrible headaches. She just couldn't hardly see with those headaches.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: I wish I could think of some things to tell you, but I can't think. Would you go down to Bow Hill very much when you lived up at the...

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Yes, we used to walk down usually on Saturday or Sunday and go to a show.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Well, sometimes in the wintertime, remember?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Yeah, we didn't go. Well, Gentry used to take it. He took us... He took us... He lived across the street. And then he took us in his buggy, too. And that's the first time I could remember the moon. I couldn't figure out that full moon was following us all the way to that show. That was the biggest thing to me. That's the only time that I remember taking a lot of notice of the moon when we was riding in that buggy. Grace and I... Oh, yes, we had our new coats on. Those little sailor coats with a little white braid. Blue and red moon and... Sound like Tara. Yeah, it's Tara and Ann.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Then we used to walk down the track a lot, you know, and we used to fish a lot up in that area. I remember one time, our mother took Grace and I and Duet and Kenneth and took us... Took a lunch, and we went up by that little river right there by Slabtown and fished, and she caught salmon. And nobody would believe that. They were up there spawning. They were up there spawning, so you know one would believe that. And she caught two of these great big salmon. And no one ever caught them since or after that. But those... It's like a sketch of a bumblebee. But it gets along, too, you know. But they were right up there in that little river, you know, where the trussle went over the railroad track. But mostly we fished with a willow stick and a string of pins. Hi, honey. Hi. This is my granddaughter. This is Ann Briggs, my granddaughter. Hi. And my little brother granddaughter, Tara.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Hi, Tara. Hi, sweetie. Hi, sweetie.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How's you?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: You've been in a lot of hair.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Haven't seen you for a long time, worldwide traveler. This is the one we can't about.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: And... And... And bumble up there. And we're not helping her very much.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was it much different once you moved into Buellville?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Well, it was a big city to us. It was a big city to us. That's what I thought.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: But, you know, on Sundays, years ago at Slabtown, when we were kids, you remember they used to ride those cows and bulls up there? Do you remember that?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Over in that field, you know, over there they used to ride... They're all little rodeos. They had little rodeos, you know, on Sunday afternoon. Just the local people, but I remember that up there, you know, because we had to stay on the outside of the bench, you know, because we were all so scared. It was very big, kind of right toward us.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: But...

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: But we had a good life in Buellville. Yeah, we had. It was always something for kids to do. You know, swimming home over there.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Full board, you know? Never lost or what to play, you know what I mean?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Not where to go or anything. It isn't like it is now.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: What am I going to do today? What can I do, you know?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: So we're... We never had any worries like that.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Where was the swimming hole?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Well, that little dried up stream, you know, that goes through there now. It wasn't dried up. It never used to dry up, you know. The more they log it off, of course, the less. And they used to... We used to dam it up over there.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: You know, that strip of houses that goes up when you're up toward Clarky Island that way, you know?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: When we'd go right across there, we lived up in that area. And we'd go across the tracks and over there in the meadow, and they would dam that up. We'd just work like men and take the sand and stuff, make sandbags and there's quite a hole there.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: And about seven or eight feet deep right there, you know?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: I mean, good diving and everything. I've never done a diver then. And then where it ran over the sacks and everything, it was this beautiful place for the little ones to wade and plaunt. And it ran all the time, so it was always fresh, you know. In that meadow, you know, as you go through Bogle on your way to Clarky, toward the end of Bogle. They used to be a big meadow in there, and the Indians used to come and camp in there, you know, when we moved down to Bogle. And there was a lot of camels. And there was a lot of camels through there, and they used to dig in. Oh, digging the camels. Of course, they always came up for Huckleberry's name. We used to have neighbors, this is Costello, and she used to live down around Cami Eye or some place down there. She'd always go out and stop them, and she knew their language, you know.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Oh, really?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Uh -huh. And she'd always go talk to them. They were always so fabulous. And we always marrowed at the little one, the little Indian children. Well, they could ride and everything, bearback, and they never had a saddle or anything.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: You were never afraid of them at all?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: No, we weren't afraid of Indians, but we were always kind of taught to be afraid of gypsies. We used to have gangs of gypsies come up through camp eight or Slabtown. And we were all, you know, they always said they stole children and then they'd haul out. Well, Mother did our two brothers that one time at Slabtown. Yeah. In fact, the total's positive because they stole boys. And they had so many of their own, I'm sure they weren't looking for anyone. I'm sure they wouldn't, but... But see those old wives' tales, and so we were always afraid of them. We were always afraid of gypsies. And they'd come around, you know, and the palm was silver to tell fortunes and things. They were always in their big wagons, horses and stuff. Oh, they were always afraid of them. But we weren't afraid of Indians. Of course, I think because we saw them from the time we were little, you know. And they were just going up to the Harkenberry patches, you know. The gypsies didn't come through them, actually. Not that much. No, but very seldom. Actually, that's all they did come through us for. Just to go house to house. And when they had made the town around on the town to tell their fortunes, they'd leave, you know. And they... Everybody said they stole things. We had to watch them. Of course, maybe they did. I don't know. I think they still watch them downtown a lot when they come in.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: They never change their style of dress or anything, have they, only here?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: They always tell them. I always have a long dress. I like the dress, you know.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: I'm fine, you know. Hmm.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What sort of things do you remember from Bowville, living there when you were young, you know, the stores that you went into, and some of the people that were around when you moved in there? Were the Bowvilles still there, or had they left?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: I guess they had. I guess they had. I guess they had.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: The Bowvilles. Yes. They had gone by that time.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Bud Noggle says that he... Bud Noggle is a brother -in -law of ours, and he says he... And his brother passed away here a while back. He said he found some old letters written to his dad, and one of them was from Mr. Bowville, asking him to sell his farm. And Earl Crane says the farm was their old farm, up above Bowville there.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Isn't that funny?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: And he still has the letters. He still has the letters. Just ran across it, you know. Oh, that was nice. He said he offered his dad $250 if he could sell that farm.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was that because he waunted to move back, or was it a promise?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: He waunted to sell a property. Sell his property, you know. In fact, they said, you see, Mr. Bowville sold so much of that meadow area. He sold it to the potlatch. And Bud said that Earl had told him that they thought they were getting the whole piece of property, but he'd held out this piece where Crane's lived. Oh. Not there for so many years, Mr. Bowville. The truth there.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: No, I don't remember the Bowville.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: We were probably a little too young to remember that anyway, you know.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: I can't think of some of the other old pioneers up there, can you?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: T .P. Joneses, we were probably all air records and everything about them. And they were awfully nice to us, so he was a superintendent of the woods there. They didn't have any children, though. And they were always awfully nice to us. That was up for ice cream and cake, a whole crowd. And they were the first ones in town to have a big sedan car that the doors would shut and didn't have those plastic push side curtains and stuff. And they used to come and stop and take a few of us for a ride. They had an eight -passenger car. They had the two jump seats, the seven -passenger, two jump seats. And oh, we were so excited to get to go for a ride in that big car. They used to have good ball games and things like that. Yeah, but they had good ball games, Bowville.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: For those every week they'd have?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Yeah. See, they had their own little leagues like they'd play with Derry and Troy. And all of them.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: And Julieta wasn't a tourist?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Yeah, and Clark, yeah. And Clark, yeah. Well, Julieta came later, I think. In later years. They probably had a good little league and everybody in town turned out, you know. But of course they'd play there one week and Clark, yeah, the next. You know, I moved around. It was a weekly deal.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Good dances. Wonderful dances. That's the joy of my life was a dance. You started pretty young in this long town, you know.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: To dancing.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Then we had Albright's Orchestra. Basket Social. Yeah, Basket Social.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Where were the dances?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Up over the Opera House.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: You know where the Opera House is in Bowville?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: That old building they called it the Opera House. The theater was downstairs.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Is that the one they're tearing down for? Are they tearing it down?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: It's been condemned for a long time. Oh no, no, no, I know. Okay. Okay, I'm going to go get it. Beautiful dance floor. Yeah, beautiful. But then it got so... Swain and sway, you know. Swain and sway. Then later they turned it into a miniature golf course up there. A miniature golf course. And they turned it into a miniature golf course. And Evelyn Denneland, her dad owned the theater and dance hall. And she was quite an artist and she drew all the pictures and murals and everything of golf and everything all around that whole hall. It's just beautiful, you know.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: They were another Pioneer family, and the Zegos. And bowls. Let's do bowls.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Neoma could probably...maybe you've gotten in touch with her.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Have you gotten in touch with Neoma Parker?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Yeah, my husband has. Uh -huh. She went to school with us. She was in our grade at school. She's Maury and she lives in Boston.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Were those dances every night? I mean every week that they had them?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Well, then they had them at Helmer sometimes. And then they'd have them at Derry. We made them at Elk River. We made them all.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: We didn't have them every week, did we?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Not there in town. No, not in the bowl. Later years, they had them pretty regularly, but not in our younger years. But we made our own dances sometimes, too. We had a club. Well, this is when I was... Well, I suppose freshman or sophomore. We had a club. We called it the Rinky Neat Club. And every teenager in town longed it. And then we'd have some kids that could play the piano or something. We'd have them come in and play for us. They'd all put in two bits or something and dance, take over the dance hall, just so we could pay them for keeping the lights on, thank you. I've heard you've probably heard about a whole light system before, Washington Water.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: We had that little delco system, you know?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: The lights went out at 11 o 'clock. They blinked the lights twice, quarter to 11. Boy, that was time. If you weren't going to go to bed, you better get the candles that we were going to hear. I don't care if he'd land.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Then they didn't come on until 10 o 'clock in the morning sometimes. His mother would get everything ready so she could run that washing machine that they had that light on or that special motor. She had to have a special motor on that first washer she had with them. We were looking at some of the old things over there, you know. I think that one was old washer. I said, this is, I give a nickel for every time we pulled up. I thought we were being in their wash. It was a little better than the washboard.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Aren't you glad you live in this age, Annie?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Yeah, I can hardly handle what I have now.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: When you gave money to the light people to turn the lights back on for you, was that?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: No, we didn't know that.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Didn't they used to do that for the movie theater?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Maybe they did, but I don't know. Maybe Mr. Davened did or something. Yeah, he may have. He may have, I don't remember that. But he had his own power plaunt for his show. Remember, I had a little old deltoid.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: There was a side, remember?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Yeah, he had that himself. Wow. But some of them may have if there was something special going on, but I don't know about that.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did the town have any, did they get together for any occasions when you were young?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Well, of course they were. They had their law educations, you know. Yeah, law, ladies' aid, all that sort of thing. And always 4th of July celebration. Always 4th of July celebration. That was a big celebration. And then the Chautauquas used to come through. Yes, that was the biggest day of their life. And the Chautauquas entertained them, which is like the tent. They've gone back to some of the tent interviews. Tent shows. They had down there in the bowl, what they call a park fair across from business. That tent would take up that whole area there. You know, it was such a big tent.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Can you describe more about them, the Chautauquas?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Because I don't know that much.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Don't you?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Well, actually it would be like Vaudeville, only it was in a tent, you know. And they'd have all kinds of different acts, all the way from opera to comic and everything, all the way, you know. And they'd always have a matinee. And they might come in for 3 or 4 days. I don't remember. I don't think they ever stayed for more than 3 days. No, I think it was about 3 days. But anyway, each show was different though. They'd have one in the afternoon and one in the evening, you know. And I was going to say, it had a big stage and well lighted. Well lighted. And they had their own. And then they had little plays sometimes. They'd get little skits, you remember, little plays. That's why I say it was just like, it was more like Vaudeville, only it was a traveling tent. Traveling Vaudeville is what it was.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What kind of things would those skits have?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: They just have, you know, like little short skits like they do when you see them on TV. You know, just...

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: I see. I can't remember it right off the hand.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: But I remember their costuming was, we thought was just beautiful. Because we'd never seen anything so glamorous as all that costuming they had. We had lots of revivals. Oh yeah. Tent revivals up there. Oh yeah.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Always have revivals.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: And then we had the circus used to come too every once in a while, remember?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Mm -hmm. Used to be down there in that meadow across from Groves down that way. Remember, we used to put it down there. Mm -hmm. And the circus.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: They looked awful big, but they probably weren't... It was a small circus. But it looked big to us. It looked big to us. The trapeze and everything. I suppose they just had one ring or something, I suppose. You know, because it wasn't...

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did they bring in animals?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Yeah. Mm -hmm.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: As I say, we thought they were wonderful, but they probably... If we see them now, we probably wouldn't think so. But your children's eyes, you know, they look pretty wonderful. Mm -hmm. Did you go to any of the revivals that they... Oh, yeah. Oh, yes. We loved singing and there's always somebody going forward to be saved.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: And they still... At that time, they still sang the same song that... The Billy Graham, the Billy Graham song. I waunt you to come forward. They still sang that old song. They sang that old song. Just as I am. Yeah, that's true. That's where we learned it. That's where we learned it, going to the revivals. I said, that's the thing I enjoy about Billy Graham's show, is that he sings all these songs that I used to hear when I was a youngster.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Would people go to them just because it was something happening? More than because it was religious or...?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: A lot of it, I think. A lot of it. I think we kids went and just have some place to go. Yeah. Probably the older people. The older people. The older people probably. Probably more serious about it. More serious about it. But you know, when you're 12 or 13 years old, you don't get much serious about things. We just love the singing. We just love singing part. We always like to press the church.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Would there be a lot of lower jumps that would come through town?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Oh, yes. Saturday night, she couldn't hardly get through the downtown of Oval. It was so filled with men coming in from the camps. And they were all so kind and good to all of us. Always nice. They only cried. All right, gently cried, we ever had. It was when they were on their WWs. Had the strike. Had the strike. And then I think hearing the grownups talk kind of put a fear in. Well, that would have noticed the downtown man through that strike, that IWW strike. I mean, there were too many. If it had just been the regular men, I don't think he'd have thought much about it. But I think it was because there were so many agitators coming in. Just like it is now. Just like it is now. They just sent agitators in to disturb the whole group. Because they needed that strike because people had to take their bedding to camp with them. And our father bring his bedding home after he'd been at camp. And mother would put it in a tub out in the backyard and pour hot water on it and just be full of bed bugs. So the IWWs, they gave him eight hours a day and cleaned beds. They only got the showers. Yeah, they got the showers. And all of that, too, they made them put in iron beds and they had these old wooden bunk beds. The lumberjacks lived in an awful, really almost an under -fireable way. And they worked hard. Yeah, they really worked hard. This is like the logging now. They worked 10 hours and they went 8 hours.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Yeah.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Were you out of the camp, son?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: No, I just bent.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: He just knows what happened. He just knows all about it. He spoke and bred. Then we used to have a lot of bums that were up there by the old water tank and the little oil tank was up above us. There used to be a lot of bums out there. I mean, they never bothered anybody. They just hobo's, you know. By the old sand house. Yeah, they used to call it the jungle. Yeah, the jungles. And they'd camp out, you know. But they didn't really bother anybody. They never bothered anybody. You can always tell when there's a bunch of them up there. They might come to town and get some groceries and things, but they really didn't bother anybody. Let's bend the paper, boy.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Who were the agitators that they were?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Were they... I think it's just people they sent from Spokane, you know, up to kind of...

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What do you don't like they do on these riots now?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: They send people into different cities to get things going, and I think they did it there. Because it was none of our local people doing it. No local people. But those lumberjacks were so kind and so good to all of us kids. Well, I was 15. I worked in lumbercage at 15. So did you. We weighed a table in the lumber camp.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Where was that?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Up at Clarkia, out of Clarkia. I grew up in the Marble Creek. We rode...

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: how far on horseback?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: I don't know. There weren't any roads that went back, and the train could only get back so far. So we rode up on the train as far as one of the camps. I don't remember which camp. Then we had to ride horseback up to the... way back into the... Way up on Grandma's Mountain. And most of us had horses that looked like horses, but she was afraid of horses, so they gave her an old white plug. And they gave one of the other girls, Laura, they gave her a little mule to ride on. And we were going around these trails way up along the river and everything, and we looked way back. And here was Sis on her little white plug and Laura. Pete's dad just loves those pictures that we had at the Marble Creek.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Is that right?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: And the pack train, and Howard Benskutter was one of the packers. He used to bring us strawberries and watermelons, because he'd known us all our life. See, they had to pack everything in there. They had to pack everything up in there on pack trains. And that wasn't that many years ago.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: That was what?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
Laura Schrager
words: Was that after the fire up there?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Well, they had that big fire. That's when we came out.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: On Marble Creek, you mean?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Yeah. That's when they had to come out. Helen and I had gone out ahead, but they had to take them out over a mountain to get them out of the tent. We had to take them out over Grandmother's... take us out over Grandmother's Mountain. And I couldn't remember what, but those Haynes were on a horse.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: And I said to the man, shall I hang on to these sticks?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: And he thought I was so stupid, because I didn't know they were horse's Haynes, but I didn't know. I know they said some of the horses, because they logged with horses then, you know. They said some of those mountains were so steep, that going up, that they had to put weights on the horse's neck to keep them, you know, balanced. Oh, that's terrible. You know, some of those mountains are straight up up there, if you've ever been up in that country. Yeah. Beautiful country, but it's true. Hi.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: So you went up there before the father, and you had to leave?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: I had to leave.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: I had already gone out. My girlfriend quit, so I quit with her. And so we went out ahead of me. Myself and Florence Greenwood and Doris Crystal, we came out on every fire. Yeah, they moved you out over the mountain. Just out over Grandmother's Mountain. I was so scared.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What was the work like that you did?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Well, it was a waiting table. We'd go to work at 5 .30 in the morning, and then we'd... Breakfast at 6. Breakfast at 6. He had to go on to work at 5. Well, Mr. Thomas and his wife had there, had things pretty well, you know.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Oh, did they?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: And then, he had the eggs and stuff, and I just had to put the coffee and the big coffee pots on the table and carry the food in, because I'd set the table. Oh, yeah, you always get that ahead of time. And then we had to mop the... Then we had, after that, we had to clean up the breakfast dish. They had all those dishes to do. There was a hundred men there, and had all those dishes to do, and all of it. A sack of onions, you peel a sack of onions, and a sack of carrots, and a sack of potatoes. The onions was the thing that got me. I just cried and cried. I couldn't hardly... They told me to hold a needle in my teeth. They told me to do everything, but I still cried. I already see.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: You worked about three shifts, right?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: I mean, split shifts about three ways. Then you'd have a couple hours off, maybe, or... Then you'd go back for lunch. Go back for lunch and serve in and out. And then you'd go back to the evening, man. You had a long day, you know. And then once a week, why, we scrubbed the floor with lye and a broom, you know, and throw buckets of water on it to rinse all the stuff off, because, you know, it had to keep it. This was a long cab and a long cookhouse that we had. And we had to scrub the floor with big buckets with the old brooms, and then rinse the water all off so that they'd be playing, you know. So they'd be... It's hard work and heavy work, you know. They had those big white porcelain plates about this, and we'd have them stack, clear up like this, when we were sitting at the table. And we used to have apple boxes of those big white cups, you know, and carry those on our hips with all the apple. And whenever somebody, like if one of the fellows quit or left, you always turn the cup up this way, and then if a stranger came in, you know where to put him. And they always ate at the same place. Nobody... Like a family. Like a family. Nobody ever sat in anybody else's place. Everybody had their own place. And another thing about Lumberjacks, they'd come in, and they were there to eat. They would have one bit of talking at the table. They came in at eight and left. They never talked or anything. I don't know whether it was some custom that they had developed or what, but they'd come in and eat and go. And in 10, 15 minutes, everybody would be gone. And you'd clean it all up again. Yeah. But let's say you work seven days a week, you know, with hardware. But you got $60 a month. Board room. Board room. And that was a lot of money on those days. That was the most you could... made more doing that than anything else.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: That ride. Terrace, this one. Did Lumberjacks give you any trouble since you were... No.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Have great respect for ladies. They had great respect for them. In fact, some of them used to come up at night and waunt to know if I waunted to walk across town when I worked at Clarkia at the ranch. Because they knew it was dark going across the meadow that they were going over to the pool room. And I waunted to go see my friend Agnes Gallagher that owned the drugstore there in Clarkia. And they'd wait until I got my dishes done. Sometimes they'd help me, and then they'd walk me over. And then when they'd get ready to go home, they'd come to the drugstore to see if I was ready to go. And then they'd walk me back home. They were all... they were wonderful. Never any trouble. They were awful nice to me.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: They never... even if they... I've heard that they would drink quite a bit even up in the... you know. They didn't do as much drinking in camp. No, not in camp. I never saw any drinking in camp. No, in camp. They would just... most of them saved their money. And then they'd come to Spokane and probably in a week's time they'd come back and were broke, you know. I said to the man I was cooking for, my... these men think you're an awfully good cook. He said, well, that's because Viola, he says that they're hungry. He said, but wait till they get their stomach all filled out good, and they'll start complaining about the food. Or either take their money and leave, you know. They really set a table on those lumber camps. Yeah, they really did. They had everything. They had a cookie.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: She's been sick? Oh, she's still on her diet?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Mom.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: When you finish, I'll tell you. When you finish, I'll tell you. Mom.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Mom. I can't think.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Would...

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Would your mother...

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Your mother would stay in town with all the kids?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Yes, she took care of the law. She was... She had a big job to do. She had 11 of us. Myself and Grace, and then... We have two brothers. And then... Just the first...

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Three brothers, right?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Three brothers, yeah, but I mean there's just four of us, and then, you know, Mabel and then all that. And then the rest of them came along. And then the rest of them came along. Dede's father was our oldest brother, the Nielsen that lives at Troy. He was our oldest brother, the one that died here a couple years ago.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you two have to help out a lot at home?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Oh, yeah. Everybody had to help out. Everybody had to help us out. In those days, there were lots of chores, though, you know, for children to do. I mean, you had to keep the old kerosene lamps cleaned and the chimneys washed and...

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Trim the wigs and fill them. And there was always wood split for the boys and things like that. I mean, there are a lot more chores than children have to do now, you know. They don't have that many.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: When I look back, we could have been a lot more helped, don't we, word of all?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: I'll tell you about it. Oh, yeah, we could. I think Mother was pretty easy on us, really, you know. Because she had to wash on the board for years. Yeah, and then, as I say, then we'd complain when we had to work the old washing machine, you know. We never came home to a cold lunch or anything, either. I don't know how she did it. Never did we ever come home to a cold lunch or anything for school. She always had super macaroni and cheese or baked beans and made wonderful, big, homemade breads, you know. Of course, all of them did and all of them did.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Our big family, our bread box, was one of those great big large containers from the lumber camps. That was our bread box. Not this high, you know. It was like a big drum.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: We had to do the dishes. I could do them on the stove. I could stand on a box by the cook stove and with my dish pan. And they put the other dish pan to drain them in down on a kitchen chair and grace that into a white foam. But that's about the only real... We never did have much argument about that, though, because I didn't like to wash them because I didn't like pots and pans. She didn't like to dry them, so we got along all right in that score. But, I mean, we didn't have too many chores to do, really. Doesn't fail. We could have helped out a lot more. Oh, yeah, a lot more. I think all children could help out a lot more. Come around and see. Thank you.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Did your father continue to...

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Stay away. You have to stay away, you know, throughout your childhood. Would you just see him on the weekend and then...

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Well, in the winter?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Yes. Well, see, our father died in 1926. So he's only 49 years old.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Doesn't sound like you could have seen that much of him when...

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: No, we really didn't. No, we didn't. No, we didn't. Because he was gone, because they worked 12 and 13 hours a day, man, you know. They didn't have any eight -hour law, eight -hour deal. He'd come home when he could, and that's all I was. Mother was always there. She was seldom ever bothered. She didn't have time to go anyplace, I don't think.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did we?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: This is going to help a sick neighbor or something.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Bye.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Is that the first time you worked out?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: We worked in that...

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Well, the first time we ever went out to work, Grace and I went picking strawberries. Yeah, if you can call that, go out to work. That was a jury act of our juries of dairy. Oh, yeah. We went down there...

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: We were down there a week, weren't we?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: And we didn't make enough money, and when we brought a crate of strawberries home, our father had to pay for it. I think it was two and a half. We didn't make our board.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: That was the first time we worked out on a job, wasn't it?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: All we did was we all slept in one bed. We made one great big long bed in their bunkhouse down there, and all we brought home was this crate of strawberries and the seven -year itch to all the kids.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: That mother really did have work. Yeah, because she had to change things every day, you know, bedding and washing and ironing. You couldn't use the sleep in the same bed, you know, you reinfected yourself. But then... We used to babysit, take care of, you know, the girls.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Why didn't you get that itch?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Well, somebody in that group, the sleeping, when we were all put our bedding and everything together, somebody in that group had it. We never did them. And they passed it along to every one of us. And then we passed it along to all the kids at home.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What was it?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: I don't even know. No, I don't think you even have it anymore. No, it's a rash that you get, and it starts like little blisters between your fingers, and it just starts itching, and then you might get it. It's like you scratch it, and then it breaks out, and it's a continuous thing to doctor to get rid of it. As I say, you don't even hear of it anymore. I don't think anybody ever has it anymore. But that was our first job. That was our first job of going out to work. We didn't do anything very exciting.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you ever let you two out of the house again to go work?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: It would have been much cheaper to keep us home. It wasn't our fault, though. I don't know. One of those things, whether I can laugh about it. Glad she could laugh about it. That mother had quite a struggle after my dad passed away, you know.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: She still had some pretty young kids?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: The youngest one was four.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: All the way up.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Were you married by then, or were you still?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: She was married by the time. When my father died. I was married in 1924. I've been married 50 years this October. So we're going to celebrate that. Going. We were just gagging at the other day. We're going to get going on it. So that's how long I've been away from home. You left then. I've been away from there for over 50 years, because I was a grandma's, you know, taking care of dad.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What, what, between the time when you worked up at Clarkville, what did you do between then and when you left?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Well, then I came up to Spokane.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Right after Clarkville?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Well, I was about to, well, I went to school in the next year, see. And then the next year, when dad was sick and grandma waunted to go east, and I came up here and stayed, you know, in east. And then that October I got married when I was 18. And then we lived over there at grandma's. But then she worked too. She worked for years and years here and now. And I used to work at Parker's Store there in Boba. I worked there for about three years. Of course, as I say, we worked when we were like 15, and we worked at the lumber camps in the summertime and then go back to school, see. And then I came to Spokane and I worked up here for a few years. And then I went back home and worked at Parker's Store for three years. Then I came back up here. I guess I haven't been up there to live for, I don't know how many years, good many years too.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: When did you work in Parker's Store?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: I can't think of the years, but it was...

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was it before the Depression?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Right at the Depression when I came up here because it was about 1927.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: You were working there when I graduated from high school?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Yeah, but I worked there before too, before. I worked there quite a while before.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: You graduated what in 31?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: 31, so it was probably, I probably worked there from maybe 28 to 31 or something like that. I know I worked there three years, three years and two days exactly. I remember. Well, I remember because I worked at the Aster here in Spokane and I went down to work at Parker's. And I worked there three years and two days and I came right back up here and started in just where I left off. I was learning to pack candy at the Aster at the time. And I came right up and they started teaching me the candy again. That's why I remember so distinctly because I thought it was so funny, that was almost exactly three years. And that was Depression time. That was just, you know... Because I worked at Cook's Nut chapter in the Depression time. And that's the reason I left Parker's because they gave my job to their son, you know, because naturally, which was right, you know. But Neelan came back then and he was having a rough time, so they put him. Because it was just general merchandise, you know, how Parker's story was. You know, measure off a yard lace and then sell a pair of logging boots, you know. Just one thing after another. But they were great people to work for. Mr. Cryer and Mr. Parker, they were wonderful people. He died to me, Mr.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Cryer.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Yeah, he cried. They're all dead now. Even Neelan, I guess.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Can you describe the inside of the Parker's story?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Oh, yes. I can almost draw you a picture.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Post office was in, down in the left -hand corner.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was that the front end of the story or the back?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Back to the back and on the left -hand side, near the back door. Here's the little post office there with all boxes, everybody's boxes and things. And the windows were around one side. And Golda Coy and Sophie, Sophie Zegel then worked in the post office at that time. And then on the left -hand side of the store, above the post office, was all the men's work paunts and the logging boots and everything like that. Then down the center aisle was yardage and lace and crochet thread and everything. And then on the far side was the groceries. They had all the groceries and things over on that side, so it's just a general merchandise story.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: That's a no -no, that's a no -no. Oh, yes.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: You bet, they get. There was another store there, the Houngroves store.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: It was another just general merchandise store. So the two of them, they were pretty good competitors.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: They sold the same kind of stuff?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Same type of thing, both of them.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: But you could, you could get a pretty good selection. But a lot of the...

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: It was pretty hard on the merchaunts, though, after people got cars and things, because they could get it cheaper by going to Moscow and things. It's kind of a lot of merchaunts there, you know. But you could get just about anything you waunted.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was the competition between the Gros store and the Parker store, was that, was it pretty friendly? I mean, were they real competitors or did they just share the business?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: No, I don't know. I don't think they were. I just think they shared the business. Yeah, I think so. Because we used to... They didn't have huge sales in Trega. Oh, no, no, no, no, nothing like that. And they both had their delivery. They both had delivery. You'd just call up and ask what you waunt, and you'd get it delivered, you know. Horse and buggy.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: So people didn't have to go to town and carry anything. Of course, as I say, that was before everybody had cars, you know. And both our brothers were delivery boys at times. And my brother Dee, that's Dee Dee's dad, you know, used to deliver them. They had to take care of the horses and everything, you know. And he was just a kid, I don't think, you know, and... 12, 13 when he was doing that.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: That was before I worked down there.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How long would that store be used during the day, do you remember?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Well, I don't think their hours were much different than they were. I think when I worked there, I worked from 9 to 6. And a couple nights a week. I think it was only one night they stayed open late.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Saturday night, wasn't it?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Saturday night when the lumberjacks came to town and everything.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Is that when a lot of lumberjacks would they show up to?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Yeah. That was their only time they could to buy, you know. No, I don't think their hours weren't any different than they are right now, or even...

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Do you remember some of the people like Pat Malone?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Oh, I should have said so. He was a real character. He was just part of the town. He was a great person. He was just part of everybody's family, I think. He was so kind and good to everybody.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: I heard he was really good to kids. He was just one of those children.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: I don't care how big they were.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: They were all kids in his eyes, you know what I mean?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Like the older boys, me 17, 18, he was nice to them. He was good to the little kids. And I can just close my eyes and see him yet. I can too, and I can see him at night. He used to say he'd get going real fast. It was like days and everybody used to say, well, Pat's got a clue. Got a clue. Pat's got a clue. He's gone. He walks so fast. Well, I think he was great for the town of old. I think he was real good. I can still see him, young Dave Ellison, sitting in the room.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Yeah, well, Pat was a real nice old man. I say there's probably a lot of others that we remember.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: If we could just think of him, you know?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: It was Billy O 'Mara. He was a nice, he was real good too.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Wasn't that type?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: No, he wasn't that type. He just worked in the city right back here. Oh, not yet.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Haven't you? Not many of the Davids left, is there?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: I think Mary and Henry are the only ones.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: They live in Lewiston, don't they?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: But I thought maybe...

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Do they live in Lewiston?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Yeah. Mary and Henry, they don't know.

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: They could probably give you a lot of... He could, because he worked on the trains and everything.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: He worked on the trains with Dad, didn't he?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
words: Breaking or something.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: And have you talked to any of the Hayses?

speaker: Viola White Cameron or Grace Ryan
